Facts.text: Pope Adrian I [Hadrian I] elected
Northern Italian towns form the Lombard League
Pasha Muhammad ibn Farukh, tyrannical governor of Jerusalem, driven out

Portugal regains independence after 60 years of Spanish rule following a revolution by Portuguese nobility; the Portuguese Restoration War begins and lasts until 1668 with recognition by Spain of the country's independence
Massachusetts becomes the first colony to give statutory recognition to slavery
An athlete from Croydon is reported to have run 20 miles from St Albans to London in less than 90 minutes
Holy Roman Emperor Ferdinand III signs an alliance with King John II Casimir Vasa of Poland against Sweden
Great Alliance occupies Brussels
Empress Elisabeth orders expulsion of all Jews from Russia
The slave ship Fredensborg sinks off Tromøy in Norway (rediscovered 1974)
Jacques Charles and Nicolas Roberts make first untethered ascension with gas hydrogen balloon in Paris
Santo Domingo (Dominican Rep) proclaims independence from Spain
Dom Pedro crowned emperor of Brazil
Erie Canal closes for entire month due to cold weather
Telegraph company opens throughout Netherlands
Raid at Stoneman: Knoxville, Tennessee to Saltville, Virginia
Skirmish at Millen Brutal, Georgia
Deputy Sheriff Elfego Baca holds off a gang of 80 Texan cowboys near Frisco, New Mexico, who want to kill him for arresting Charles McCarty
Sino-Portuguese treaty recognizes Portugal's control of Macao
1st certified public accountants receive certificates (NY)
"The Great Train Robbery" the 1st Western film, released starring Justus D. Barnes and G. M. Anderson
German Shoemaker Wilhelm Voigt (Capt of Köpenick) sentenced to 4 years for forgery after posing as Prussian officer
1st Israeli kibbutz founded, Deganya Alef
First Christmas Club payment is made to Carlisle Trust Company of Pennsylvania

Boston Braves MLB franchise owner James Gaffney buys the Allston Golf Club on Commonwealth Avenue, Boston with a plan to construct a ball park there; ground breaking for Braves Field starts on March 20, 1915
1st drive-up gasoline station opens in Pittsburgh
Flag of Greece officially raised at Firka Fortress, Chania Crete symbolising the union of Crete and Greece
The US requests that Germany withdraw its military and naval attaches from the Embassy in Washington
Boys Town founded by Father Edward Flanagan west of Omaha, Nebraska
Danish parliament passes an act to grant Iceland independence under Danish crown
Yugoslavia declares independence with a monarchy proclaimed in Belgrade
AA Milne's comedy play "Mr Pim Passes By" premieres in Manchester
1st US helium-filled dirigible makes its 1st flight
US Post Office establishes philatelic agency
1st skywriting over US - "Hello USA"-by Capt Turner, RAF
Boston Bruins beat fellow expansion team Montreal Maroons, 2-1 at Boston Arena; first NHL game to be played in the United States
Plutarco Elías Calles becomes President of Mexico
Peace Treaty of Locarno signed between Germany, France, Belgium, Great Britain, and Italy

National League President John Heydler first to propose a baseball rule change calling for a 10th man, or 'designated hitter', to bat in place of the pitcher; ironically, the NL vote in favour of proposal, but the American League turn it down
Railroad museum opens in Utrecht, Netherlands
Game of Bingo invented by American toy salesman Edwin S. Lowe
NHL drops 20 minute slashing-about-the-head penalty
Ottawa branch of Royal Mint begins operation as Royal Canadian Mint
Austria has world's 1st Day of Postage Stamp
2nd Heisman Trophy Award: Larry Kelley, Yale (E)
Bell Labs tests coaxial cable for TV use
Ernest Brundin & Frank Lyon obtain US patent for hydroponic (soil-less) culture of plants
School bus & train collide in Salt Lake City Utah
Four sets of brothers play in one NHL game when Chicago Blackhawks beat NY Rangers, 4-1; Lynn & Muzz Patrick and Neil & Mac Colville (Rangers); Max & Doug Bentley and Bob & Bill Carse (Chicago)
British cruiser HMS Dorsetshire forces crew of German submarine supply ship MV Python to scuttle vessel in the South Atlantic 1,150 miles west of South Africa
US Civil Air Patrol (CAP) organizes
Gasoline rationed in US

With WWII travel restrictions in mind, MLB owners decide to restrict travel to a 3-trip schedule rather than customary 4; Spring training in 1943 limited to locations north of Potomac or Ohio rivers and east of the Mississippi
At the end of the Tehran Conference, the Big Three (Stalin, Churchill and Roosevelt) agree that the invasion of Normandy should take place in May 1944
Mail routing resumes in free South Netherlands
Arabic Congress names Abdullah of Trans Jordan as King of Palestine
Piet Roozenburg becomes world champion checker player
MLB announces attendance for the season is 20.2 million, down from 20.9 in 1948; New York Yankees and the Cleveland Indians each finish with over 2.2 million, but the St. Louis Browns fall to 270,000
WBNG TV channel 12 in Binghamton, NY (CBS) begins broadcasting
WKTV TV channel 2 in Utica, NY (NBC) begins broadcasting
17th Heisman Trophy Award: Dick Kazmaier, Princeton (HB)
Golden Gate Bridge closes due to high winds
The New York Daily News reports the first successful sex reassignment surgery

Red Sox trade for slugger Jackie Jensen, sending pitcher Mickey McDermott and outfielder Tom Umphlett to Washington; Jensen averages 25 HRs a year for his 7 seasons in Boston; AL RBI leader x 3, and AL MVP in 1958
WAIM (now WAXA) TV channel 40 in Anderson, SC (IND) 1st broadcast
WCSH TV channel 6 in Portland, ME (NBC) begins broadcasting
Nationalist China & US sign dike agreement

NY Yankees and Baltimore Orioles complete largest trade in MLB history as 17 players, includingDon Larsen, Gene Woodling, Bob Turley change teams; first phase of transaction began November 18 and concludes today after the MLB draft
Algerian-born French long-distance runner Alain Mimoun wins the men's marathon in 2:25:00.0 at the Melbourne Olympics; first time runners follow painted line
American Mildred McDaniel jumps world record 1.76m to win the women's high jump gold medal at the Melbourne Olympics; Briton Thelma Hopkins and Russian Mariya Pisareva dead-heat for silver (1.67m)

Hungarian boxer László Papp wins his 3rd straight Olympic gold medal when he beats future Hall of Famer José Torres representing the US on points in the light-middleweight final at the Melbourne Olympics

In front of 100,000 fans at the Melbourne Cricket Ground, a US Army baseball team beats an Australian all-star team, 11–5 in an Olympic exhibition game; Sergeant Vance Sutton belts a grand slam for Army
Indonesian VP Mohammed Hatta resigns
Romanian canoeist Leon Rotman wins the men's C-1,000m gold medal at the Melbourne Olympics; claims the singles double after also winning the C-1 10,000m gold
Central African Republic made autonomous member of the French Community (National Day)

Liberty Records releases single "The Chipmunk Song (Christmas Don't Be Late)" by David Seville and the Chipmunks; it becomes a #1 hit, and wins 3 Grammy Awards (Comedy Performance, Children's Recording, and Best Engineered),
Our Lady of Angels School fire kills 92 students & 3 nuns in Chicago, Illinois
12 nations sign treaty for scientific peaceful use of Antarctica
The 1st color photograph of Earth received from outer space
The independent Republic of West Papua is proclaimed in Western New Guinea

Classifications in minor league baseball are overhauled; Eastern and South Atlantic leagues are promoted from Class-A to Class-AA; classes B, C and D are abolished with those leagues being promoted to Class-A
KGMB TV channel 9 in Honolulu, HI (CBS) begins broadcasting
Nagaland becomes a state of the Indian union
Wendell Scott wins the Grand National Series Jacksonville 200 at Speedway Park in Jacksonville, Florida, becoming the first black driver to win a race at NASCAR's premier level
After just 3 seasons in MLB the Houston Colt .45s change name of the team to Astros; owners say move signals a step into the future for the franchise and the city of Houston
Airlift of refugees from Cuba to US begins
South Africa's government says children of white fathers are white

Ecuadorian national baseball team defends its title with a 4-3 win over Brazil in the South American Championship; last win for next 50 years; Eloy Guerrero drives in Ramón Sotomayor with the winning run
Georg Kiesinger elected West German chancellor
Housing charity Shelter launches in Britain
Radio time signal WWV moves from Greenbelt, Maryland to Boulder, Colorado
Pacific Northwest Sports, Inc. is awarded one of the 2 American League baseball expansion franchises; new team named the Seattle Pilots
Seattle awarded one of the 2 AL expansion franchise teams
Gonzalo Barrios, Venezuelan presidential candidate
LAPD Police Chief Edward Davis announces warrants for the arrest of the Manson cult for murder
US government holds its 1st draft lottery since WW II
Independent People's Republic of South Yemen renames itself as People's Democratic Republic of Yemen
Luis Echeverria Alvarez sworn in as president of Mexico
Khmer Rouge rebels intensify assaults on Cambodian government positions, forcing their retreat from Kompong Thmar and nearby Ba Ray
2 people killed and 127 injured when 2 car bombs explode in the centre of Dublin, Ireland
Wings release "Hi, Hi, Hi" in UK
Australia grants self-government to Papua New Guinea
American Jacqueline Hansen runs female world record marathon 2:43:54.5 in Culver City, California
Boeing 727 crashes in Upperville, Virginia, killing 92
LA Skid Row slasher kills 1st of 8
"The Robert MacNeil Report", later "The MacNeil-Lehrer Report", currently known as "PBS NewsHour" program premieres nationally in US on PBS
Kuwait and Gulf and BP agree on terms of nationalization
Angola admitted to the UN
US performs nuclear test at Nevada Test Site
46th Heisman Trophy Award: George Rogers, South Carolina (RB)
Mel Harris appears on M*A*S*H in "Cementing Relationships"
US Justice Department sues Yonkers siting racial discrimination
Yugoslavian charter flight crashes into Mont San-Pietro in Corsica, 180 killed
Dentist Barney B Clark gets 1st artificial heart
Miguel de la Madrid inaugurated as President of Mexico
American boxer Greg Page KOs home town favourite Gerrie Coetzee in 8th round to win WBA heavyweight title in Sun City, South Africa
France performs nuclear test
Noraly Beyer becomes the Netherland's 1st black TV newscaster
South Africa's Cosatu union centre forms
STS 61-C vehicle moves to launch pad
Musée d'Orsay opens in Paris
Paul McCartneyreleases single "Only Love Remains"
Digging begins to link England & France under English Channel
596 dead after cyclone hits Bangladesh, half a million homeless
Chinese minister of Foreign affairs Qian Qichen visits Moscow
First World AIDS day to raise awareness of the AIDS global epidemic
NBC bids a record $401 million to capture television broadcasting rights for the 1992 Barcelona Summer Olympic Games
"Day Without Art" - Artists demonstrate against AIDS
East Germany drops communist monopoly from its constitution
Mark Langston signs record $3.2 million per year Cal Angels contract
56th Heisman Trophy Award: Ty Detmer, Brigham Young (QB)
Iraq accepts Bush's offer for talks
Lithuania, Estonia & Latvia hold their 1st joint session
92% of Ukrainians vote for independence from the Soviet Union
Colorado party wins Paraguay parliamentary election
US 75th manned space mission "STS 44" Atlantis 10 lands
Charlemagnejudges the accusations against Pope Leo III in the Vatican
Henry Vof England enters Paris
Franz Liszt, aged 11, debuts as a pianist in Vienna
US House of Representatives begins to decide outcome of election deadlock betweenJohn Quincy AdamsandAndrew Jackson(Adams wins)
Alexandra Feodorovna[Princess Charlotte of Prussia] becomes Empress consort when Tsar Nicholas I ascends the throne
Henry John Heinz's first company, the Heinz Noble & Company, goes bankrupt in the aftermath of the Panic of 1873
Society of Independent Artists hold 1st exhibition in Polychrome Pavilion, Paris, includesGeorges Seurat's "Bathers at Asnières"
Sherlock Holmes first appears in print in "Study in Scarlet" byArthur Conan Doyle
Exiled South African President of TransvaalPaul Krugervisits Flanders and on the same day is declined a visit from the German Kaiser
Porfirio Díazis inaugurated for his 6th consecutive term as President of Mexico
There is virtual civil war in Greece as royalists fightEleftherios Venizelos' Liberal Party, the Allies ensure a Venizelist victory
LadyNancy Astorsworn-in as 1st female member of British Parliament
Polish state Chief MarshalJózef Piłsudskiresigns
"La Révolution surréaliste" publishes its first issue in Paris, edited byAndré Breton
Rudolf Hess& Ernest Rohm become ministers inHitler's government
Leningrad mayor Sergei Kirov is assassinated,Joseph Stalinuses it as an excuse to begin his Great Purge of 1934-38
Toronto Maple Leafs beat St. Louis Eagles, 4-3 to set an NHL record for the most wins to start the season with 8; Leafs do it again 59 years later; won 10 in a row to start 1993-94 season
Japan recognizes Spain's Franco government
SS-FuhrerHimmlerbegins deportation of Polish Jews
JapaneseEmperor Hirohitosigns declaration of war
TheBeveridge Reportis published by the British government unveiling plans for a post-war welfare state
Béla Bartòk's "Concerto for Orchestra" premieres in Symphony Hall, Boston, with the Boston Symphony Orchestra conducted by Serge Koussevitzky
Benjamin Britten's opera "Billy Budd" premieres in London
Hugh Hefnerpublishes the first edition of Playboy magazine, featuringMarilyn Monroeas the magazine's first centerfold
Rosa Parksis arrested for refusing to move to the back of a bus and give her seat to a white passenger in Montgomery, Alabama

Australian women's 4 x 100m relay team of Norma Croker,Betty Cuthbert, Fleur Mellor & Shirley Strickland de la Hunty run world record 44.65 to win the gold medal at the Melbourne Olympics; Cuthbert's 3rd gold of the Games
Frank Robinson(NL) & Luis Aparicio (AL) voted Rookie of the Year
Gert Fredrikssonof Sweden wins his 3rd consecutive K-1 1,000m canoeing gold medal by 2.5s from Igor Pissarov of the Soviet Union at the Melbourne Olympics; also wins K-1 10,000m gold
Led by future Basketball Hall of FamersBill Russelland K.C. Jones, the US wins it's 4th consecutive Olympic gold medal with an 89-55 victory over the Soviet Union at the Melbourne Games
Legendary Australian swimmerDawn Frasersets world record 1:02.0 to win the women's 100m freestyle at the Melbourne Olympics; first of Fraser's 3 consecutive gold medals in the event
Musical comedy film "The Girl Can't Help It" starringJayne Mansfieldwith cameos by rock 'n' roll starsLittle Richard, Eddie Cochran, and Gene Vincent premieres
US men's 4 x 100m relay team of Thane Baker, Leamon King,Bobby Morrow& Ira Murchison sets world record 39.60s to win the gold medal at the Melbourne Olympics; Morrow's 3rd gold medal of the Games

Seminal rock bandBuddy Holly& The Crickets make their "Ed SullivanShow" debut, performing "That'll Be The Day" and "Peggy Sue"; singerSam Cookealso performs after getting bumped due to time constraints on November 3rd - he sings "You Send Me" and “(I Love You) For Sentimental Reasons”
25th Heisman Trophy Award:Billy Cannon, LSU (HB)
British rockerPaul McCartneyand drummerPete Bestarrested and then deported from Hamburg, Germany accused of attempted arson
Congolese independence leaderPatrice Lumumbais caught in Lodi, Congo, by troops of recent coup leaderJoseph-Désiré Mobutu
Martin Luther King Jr.speaks toJ. Edgar Hooverabout his slander campaign
Philadelphia centerWilt Chamberlainscores 52 points in 76ers' 133-109 win over Seattle SuperSonics; sets NBA record for 22 free throw misses
Queen Elizabeth IIinaugurates 98-inch (249-cm) Isaac Newton telescope
Track Records releases theJimi HendrixExperience's 2nd studio album "Axis: Bold as Love" in the UK, just seven months after their debut release

Chicago Cubs release longtime star and future Hall of FamerErnie Banks, ending his 19-year MLB career; announce Banks will serve as a coach on managerLeo Durocher's staff; Banks finishes with 512 home runs and 1,636 RBI
John LennonandYoko Onorelease single "Happy Xmas (War is Over)" in the US
Jack Nicklausfinishes at 13-under-par 275 to win the Walt Disney World Open by 1 stroke over Mason Rudolph and becomes the first player to reach $2 million in PGA Tour career earnings
US PresidentGerald Fordvisits People's Republic of China
Bangladeshi Army Chief of StaffZiaur Rahmandeclares himself Chief Martial Law Administrator and de facto leader
Sex Pistolsuse profanity on TV, getting them branded as "rotten punks"

Australian cricket fast bowler Rodney Hogg debuts in 1st Test v England in Brisbane; after Australia all out 116, Hogg makes instant impact by dismissing both high profile England openersGraham Gooch(2) and Geoff Boycott (13)
US PresidentJimmy Cartermore than doubles national park system size
Kareem Abdul-Jabbarpasses Oscar Robertson (26,710) to become the NBA’s second all-time leading scorer behind Wilt Chamberlain; scores 14 points in 117-86 Lakers' win over Utah Jazz in Los Angeles
"Tootsie" directed bySydney Pollackand starringDustin HoffmanandJessica Langepremieres in Hollywood
"Beverly Hills Cop" directed by Martin Brest and starringEddie Murphyand Judge Reinhold premieres in Los Angeles
50th Heisman Trophy Award:Doug Flutie, Boston College (QB)
TV mini series "Anne of Green Gables" based on the novel byLucy Maud Montgomery, starring Megan Follows is first shown on CBS in Canada
Benazir Bhuttois named Prime Minister of Pakistan, the first female leader of a Muslim country
Roy Orbisongives his final concert at The Front Row Theater in Cleveland, Ohio
Romanian 5-time Olympic gold medal winning gymnastNadia Comăneciarrives in NYC requesting political asylum to the United States; granted
USSR PresidentMikhail Gorbachevmeets PopeJohn Paul IIat the Vatican
Dictator of ChadHissène Habréflees to Cameroon
New York Knicks centerPatrick Ewingscores career high 22 field goals in his 50 point haul in 113-96 win v Charlotte at Madison Square Garden
Miami quarterbackDan Marinosets an NFL record when he reaches 3,000 yards passing for the 8th time in his career during Dolphin's 33-14 win over Tampa Bay at Joe Robbie Stadium
Nursultan Nazarbayevsworn in as president of Kazakhstan

Reigning US Open championPayne Stewartwins only 1 hole, but claims the Skins Game at La Quinta and sets record in the process; 3-foot birdie putt at 14th hole earns 8 skins worth $260,000; record for most money won on 1 hole
Ford Motor Company institutes the world's first moving assembly line for the Model T Ford
British and French workers meet in the middle of the Channel Tunnel under the English Channel
Louis VI 'The Fat', King of the Franks (1108-37), born in Paris (d. 1137)
Anna Comnena, Byzantine princess and historian (Alexiad), born in the Great Palace of Constantinople, Byzantine Empire (d. 1153)
Takeda Shingen, Japanese warlord of Kai Province (1541-73) known as the "Tiger of Kai", born in Kai Province, Japan (d. 1573)
Tadeáš Hájek, Czech naturalist, physician and astronomer, born in Prague, Kingdom of Bohemia (d. 1600)
Johan van der Veeken, Dutch merchant, richest shipowner and banker in Rotterdam, born in Mechelen (d. 1616)
Philip of Nassau, Count of Nassau, Katzenelnbogen, Vianden and Dietz, fought for the United Provinces (Eighty Years' War), born in Dillenburg, Germany (d. 1695)
Nicolas-Claude Fabri de Peiresc, French astronomer and antiquarian, born in Belgentier, France (d. 1637)
Jan-Erasmus Quellinus [Quellien], Flemish painter (court painter to Emperor Leopold I), born in Antwerp (d. 1715)
Francesco Stradivari, Italian violin maker and son of Antonius, born in Cremona, Duchy of Milan, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1743)
John Keill, Scottish mathematician, born in Edinburgh, Scotland, (d. 1721)
Philip Yorke, 1st Earl of Hardwicke, Lord Chancellor of England, born in Dover, Kent (d. 1764)
Franz Xaver Richter, Austro-Moravian composer, singer and conductor, born in Holleschau, Moravia (d. 1789) [probable place of birth]
Bernhard Christian Weber, German composer, born in Wolferschwenda, Electorate of Saxony, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1758)
Etienne-Maurice Falconet, French sculptor patronized by Madame de Pompadour, born in Paris, France (d. 1791)
Anna Louisa Karsch "Die Karschin", German poet, born in Hammer, Silesia (d. 1791)
Dismas Hatas, Czech composer, born in Vysoké Mýto, Kingdom of Bohemia (d. 1777)
Eggert Ólafsson, Icelandic explorer, writer (Reise igiennem Island - Travels In Iceland), and language conservationist, born in Svefneyjar, Iceland (d. 1768)
Giuseppe Sarti, Italian composer, baptized in Faenza, Papal States (d. 1802)
Martin Heinrich Klaproth, German chemist (discovered uranium), born in Wernigerode, Brandenburg (d. 1817)
Pyotr Ivanovich Turchaninov, Russian church composer and choirmaster, born in Kiev, Russian Empire (d. 1856)
Charles Philippe Lafont, French composer and violinist, born in Paris, France (d. 1839)
Castil-Blaze [François Henri Joseph Blaze], French composer and music critic, born in Cavaillon, France (d. 1857)
Pavel Ivanovich Dolgorukov, Russian composer, born in Moscow, Russia (d. 1845)
Nikolai Lobachevsky, Russian mathematician (Lobachevskian geometry), born in Nizhny Novgorod (d. 1856)
Lungtok Gyatso, 9th Dalai Lama, born near Dan Chokhor Monastery (d. 1815)
Joseph Gungl, Austro-Hungarian bandleader and composer of dances and marches, born in Zsámbék, Hungary (d. 1889)
August Rockel, German composer, born in Graz, Austria (d. 1876)
Ernest Reyer, French opera composer, born in Marseilles, France (d. 1909)
William Mahone, American railway engineer and Major General (Confederate Army), born near Courtland, Virginia (d. 1895)
Archibald Gracie III, American army officer, Brigadier General (Confederate Army), born in New York City (d. 1864)
Micah Jenkins, American Brigadier General (Confederate Army), born in Edisto Island, South Carolina (d. 1864)
Alexandra, Danish princess, Queen of Great Britain and Ireland, born in Copenhagen, Denmark (d. 1925)
Alfred Cellier, British conductor (Glibert and Sullivan) and composer (Dorothy), born in South Hackney, London (d. 1891)
Agathe Backer Grøndahl, Norwegian pianist, composer, and teacher, born in Holmestrand, Norway (d. 1907)
Peter Lange-Müller, Danish composer and pianist, born in Frederiksberg, Denmark (d. 1926)
Eligiusz Niewiadomski, Polish modernist painter and assassin of Poland's first President, Gabriel Narutowicz, born in Warsaw, Congress Poland, Russian Empire (d. 1923)
Archie MacLaren, English cricket batsman and captain (35 Tests; 424 Lancashire v Somerset 1895), born in Manchester, England (d. 1944)
Charles Ruijs de Beerenbrouck, Dutch politician Prime Minister of the Netherlands (1918-25, 29-33), born in Roermond, Netherlands (d. 1936)
Dominicus Johner (né Franz-Xaver Johner), German composer and Gregorian chant scholar, born in Waldsee, Kingdom of Prussia (d. 1955)
Akiba Rubinstein, Polish chess player (openings theorist), born in Stawiski, Congress Poland (d. 1961)
Romanos Melik'yan, Armenian composer, born in Kizlyar,  the Terek Oblast, Russian Empire (d. 1935)
Karl Schmidt-Rottluff, German expressionist painter and print maker (Die Brücke founder), born in Rottluff, German Empire (d. 1976)
Torben Myer, Danish actor (Girl Who Came Bac; Judgment at Nuremberg; Sullivan's Travels), born in Århus, Denmark (d. 1975)
Guy de Lioncourt, French composer, born in Caen, France (d. 1961)
Jack Crawford, English cricket all-rounder (12 Tests, 2 x 50, 39 wickets; Surrey CCC, South Australia), born in Cane Hill, Surrey, England (d. 1963)
Pierre Kemp, Dutch poet (Fugitives & Constants), born in Maastricht, Netherlands (d. 1967)
Rex Stout, American mystery writer (Nero Wolf novels), born in Noblesville, Indiana (d. 1975)
Zhu De, Chinese general and politician (commander-in-chief of Eighth Route Army), born in Yilong Country, Qing Dynasty (d. 1976)
Charlie Bachman, American College Football Hall of Fame guard and coach (Notre Dame, Florida, Michigan State), born in Chicago, Illinois (d. 1985)
Ernst Toller, German playwright, President of Bavarian Soviet Republic (1919), born in Samotschin (d. 1939)
Herman Griffith, West Indian cricket fast bowler (13 Tests; 44 wickets; best 6-103), born in Arima, Trinidad and Tobago (d. 1980)
Henry Williamson, English author (Tarka the Otter), born in Bedfordshire, England (d. 1977)
Petko Staynov, Bulgarian composer, born in Kazanlak, Bulgaria (d. 1977)
Cyril Ritchard, Australianactor(Peter Pan; Hans Brinker), born in Sydney, New South Wales (d. 1977)
Robert W. Welch Jr., American businessman who founded the conservative John Birch Society, born in Chowan County, North Carolina (d. 1985)
Dorothy James, American composer (Paola and Francesca; Patterns), and educator, born in Chicago, Illinois (d. 1982)
Ilona Fehér, Hungarian-Israeli violinist, and teacher, born in Budapest, Hungary (d. 1988)
Red Badgro, American Pro Football Hall of Fame end and coach (NY Giants) and baseball outfielder (St. Louis Browns), born in Orillia, Washington (d. 1998)
"Tony" W. A. Boyle, American United Mine Workers president convicted of murder, born in Bald Butte, Montana (d. 1985)
Charles G. Finney, American fantasy author (Circus of Dr Lao), born in Sedalia, Missouri (d. 1984)
Ida Carroll, English double bassist, educator, administrator (Northern School of Music, 1956-72), and composer, born in West Didsbury, England (d. 1995)
Jean Cartan, French composer, born in Nancy, France (d. 1932)
Alex Wilson, Canadian athlete (Olympic silver 800m, 2 x bronze 400m, 4x400m relay 1932), born in Montreal, Quebec (d. 1994)
Joel Fluellen, American actor (Porgy and Bess, Burning Cross, Learning Tree), born in Monroe, Louisiana (d. 1990)
Georgios Kasassoglou, Greek violinist, composer (Four Preludes on The Return from The Front; Clouds), educator and arts advocate, born in Athens, Greece (d. 1984)
Jan Koplowitz, German writer, born in Kudowa (d. 2001)
Alicia Markova, English ballerina and choreographer (Diaghilev's Ballet sRusses 1925-32), born in London (d. 2004)
Louis Slotin, Canadian Physicist and Chemist (Manhattan Project, Los Alamos) who assembled the plutonium core for 'Trinity', the first detonated atomic device, born in Winnipeg, Manitoba (d. 1946)
Calvin Griffith, Canadian baseball team owner (Washington Senators/Minnesota Twins 1955-84), born in Montreal, Quebec (d. 1999)
Franz Binder, Austrian soccer striker and manager (19 caps Austria, 9 caps Germany; Rapid Vienna), born in St. Pölten, Austria (d. 1989)
Cookie Lavagetto, American baseball third baseman (MLB All Star 1938-41; Pittsburgh Pirates, Brooklyn Dodgers) and manager (Washington Senators 1957-61), born in Oakland, California (d. 1990)
Ken Willingham, English soccer right half (12 caps; Huddersfield Town), born in Ecclesfield, Sheffield, England (d. 1975)
Minoru Yamasaki, American architect (World Trade Center, NYC), born in Seattle, Washington (d. 1986)
Terence Beckles, English pianist and teacher, born in Bushey, England (d. 1995)
Johnny Johnston, American singer (Make that Spare), born in St Louis, Missouri (d. 1996)
Wan Li, Chinese reform politician, born in Dongping County, Shandong province (d. 2015)
Geraldine McCullough, American painter and sculptor (Phoenix), born in Maywood, Illinois (d. 2008)
Marty Marion, American baseball shortstop and manager (MLB All-Star 1943–50; NL MVP 1944; St. Louis Cardinals), born in Richburg, South Carolina (d. 2011)
William Tracy, American character actor (To the Shores of Tripoli; The Shop Around the Corner), born in Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania (d. 1967)
Bill Strannigan, American all-round athlete and basketball coach (Uni of Wyoming), born in Dalry, North Ayrshire, Scotland (d. 1997)
Anne Cox Chambers, American media owner (Cox Enterprises) and US ambassador to Belgium (1977-81), born in Dayton, Ohio (d. 2000)
Ike Isaacs, Burmese-English jazz guitarist, born in Rangoon, Burma (d. 1996)
Lurlean Hunter, American jazz singer, born in Clarksdale, Mississippi (d. 1983)
John Bunch, American jazz pianist (New York Swing Trio), born in Tipton, Indiana (d. 2010)
Ralph Manza, American actor (Banacek, Mama Malone, Newhart), born in San Francisco, California (d. 2000)
Paul Picerni, American actor (The Untouchables), born in New York City (d. 2011)
Vsevolod Bobrov, Soviet ice hockey left wing (Olympic gold 1956) and soccer striker (3 caps), born in Morshansk, Tambov, Russia (d. 1979)
Morris [Maurice De Bevere], Belgian cartoonist (Lucky Luke), born in Kortrijk, Belgium (d. 2001)
Stansfield Turner, American admiral and CIA director (1977-81), born in Highland Park, Illinois (d. 2018)
Harold Strachan, South African writer, anti-apartheid activist, and explosives expert, born in Pretoria, Union of South Africa (d. 2020)
Jaime Mendoza-Nava, Bolivian-American composer and conductor, born in La Paz, Bolivia (d. 2005)
Jordan Klein, American underwater photographer, cinematographer and director (Thunderball), born in Miami, Florida (d. 2024)[1][2]
Martin Rodbell, American biochemist (1994 Nobel Prize for medicine or Physiology for discovery of G-proteins), born in Baltimore, Maryland (d. 1998)
Keith Michell, Australian actor and theater director (6 Wives of Henry VIII), born in Adelaide, South Australia (d. 2015)
Robert Symonds, American actor and associate director of the Repertory Theater of Lincoln Center, born in Bristow, Oklahoma (d. 2007)
Grant Beglarian, Armenian-American composer (Of Fables, Foibles, and Fancies), educator (University of Southern California, 1969-86), and arts administrator, born in Tiflis, Georgia (d. 2002)
David Doyle, American actor (Charlie's Angels, Rugrats), born in Omaha, Nebraska (d. 1997)
Dick Shawn [Richard Schulefand], American character actor and comedian (Producers, Maid to Order, Angel), born in Buffalo, New York (d. 1987)
Emily McLaughlin, American actress (Jessie Brewer in "General Hospital"), born in White Plains, New York (d. 1991)
Leon Biriotti, Uruguayan composer and oboist, born in Montevideo, Uruguay (d. 2020)
Joachim Hoffmann, German historian and academic director of the German Armed Forces Military History Research Office, born in Königsberg, East Prussia (d. 2002)
